THE GENERATOR POLYNOMIAL OF A CYCLIC CODE
The following theorem contains some basic facts about cyclic codes. It says, among other things, that the ring $\mathbf{R}_{\mathbf{n}}$ is a principal ideal domain.
Theorem 7.4.1 Let $C$ be an ideal in $R_n$ , that is, a cyclic code of length $n$ .

1) There is a unique monic polynomial $\mathbf{g}(\mathbf{x})$ of minimum degree in C. This polynomial generates C, that is, $\mathbf{C} = \langle \mathbf{g}(\mathbf{x}) \rangle$ , and it is called the generator polynomial for C. (We remark that the generator polynomial is usually not the only polynomial that generates C.)
3) If $\deg(g(x)) = r$ , then $C$ has dimension $n - r$ . In fact,
(1) If $\mathbf{g}(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{g}_0 + \mathbf{g}_1\mathbf{x} + \dots + \mathbf{g}_r\mathbf{x}^r$ , then $\mathbf{g}_0 \neq 0$ and C has generator matrix

$$
G = \left[ \begin{array}{c c c c c c c c c c} g _ {0} & g _ {1} & g _ {2} & \dots & & g _ {r} & 0 & 0 & \dots & 0 \\ 0 & g _ {0} & g _ {1} & g _ {2} & \dots & & g _ {r} & 0 & \dots & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & g _ {0} & g _ {1} & g _ {2} & \dots & & g _ {r} & & \vdots \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \ddots & \ddots & \ddots & & & \ddots & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & \dots & 0 & g _ {0} & g _ {1} & g _ {2} & \dots & & g _ {r} \end{array} \right]
$$
where each row of $\mathbf{G}$ is a cyclic shift of the previous row.

In the previous example, we saw that $\mathbf{C} = \langle 1 + \mathbf{x} \rangle = \langle 1 + \mathbf{x}^2 \rangle$ . It will be convenient to adopt the notation $\mathbf{C} = \langle \mathbf{p}(\mathbf{x}) \rangle$ to denote the fact that $\mathbf{C}$ is the ideal generated by $\mathbf{p}(\mathbf{x})$ and that $\mathbf{p}(\mathbf{x})$ is the generator polynomial for $\mathbf{C}$ .

Let $\mathfrak{p}(\mathbf{x})$ be a monic polynomial in $\mathbf{R}_{\mathbf{n}}$ , and let $\mathbf{C} = \langle \mathfrak{p}(\mathbf{x}) \rangle$ be the cyclic code generated by that polynomial. If $\mathbf{p}(\mathbf{x})$ does not divide $\mathbf{x}^{\mathbf{n}} - 1$ , then according to the previous theorem, it cannot be the generator polynomial for C. On the other hand, the next theorem shows that if $\mathfrak{p}(\mathbf{x})$ does divide $\mathbf{x}^{\mathbf{n}} - 1$ , then it is the generator polynomial for C.

Theorem 7.4.2 A monic polynomial $\mathbf{p}(\mathbf{x})$ in $\mathbf{R}_{\mathbf{n}}$ is the generator of a polynomial for a cyclic code if and only if $\mathbf{p}(\mathbf{x}) \mid \mathbf{x}^{\mathbf{n}} - 1$ .

Proof. We have already established one implication. As to the converse, suppose that $\mathbf{p}(\mathbf{x}) \mid \mathbf{x}^{\mathbf{n}} - 1$ , and let $\mathbf{g}(\mathbf{x})$ be the generator polynomial for $\mathbf{C} = \langle \mathbf{p}(\mathbf{x}) \rangle$ . Assume that $\mathbf{p}(\mathbf{x}) \neq \mathbf{g}(\mathbf{x})$ . Since $\mathbf{p}(\mathbf{x})$ and $\mathbf{g}(\mathbf{x})$ are both monic, we must have $\deg (\mathbf{p}(\mathbf{x})) > \deg (\mathbf{g}(\mathbf{x}))$ .

Theorems 7.4.1 and 7.4.2 tell us that the map $\phi: \mathbf{g}(\mathbf{x}) \to \langle \langle \mathbf{g}(\mathbf{x}) \rangle \rangle$ , which sends each monic divisor $\mathbf{g}(\mathbf{x})$ of $\mathbf{x}^{\mathbf{n}} - 1$ to the cyclic code $\langle \langle \mathbf{g}(\mathbf{x}) \rangle \rangle$ , is a one-to-one correspondence between the set $\mathfrak{D}_{\mathbf{n}}$ of all monic divisors of $\mathbf{x}^{\mathbf{n}} - 1$ and the set $\mathfrak{J}_{\mathbf{n}}$ of all cyclic codes in $\mathbb{R}_{\mathbf{n}}$ . This explains why is it important to be able to factor the polynomial $\mathbf{x}^{\mathbf{n}} - 1$ over a finite field.

Theorem 7.4.3 Let $C_1 = \langle \mathbf{g}_1(\mathbf{x}) \rangle$ and $C_2 = \langle \mathbf{g}_2(\mathbf{x}) \rangle$ be cyclic codes in $R_{\mathfrak{n}}$ . Then

3) $C_1 + C_2 = \langle \gcd(\mathbf{g}_1(x), \mathbf{g}_2(x)) \rangle$ .

The representation of a cyclic code by its zeros can be used to show that some Hamming codes are cyclic codes. The binary case is the easiest, so let us consider it first.
Theorem 7.4.7 The binary Hamming code $\mathfrak{H}_2(\mathbf{r})$ is equivalent to a cyclic code.

Proof. Recall that the binary Hamming code $\mathfrak{H}_2(\mathbf{r})$ has parameters $[2^{\mathbf{r}} - 1, 2^{\mathbf{r}} - 1 - \mathbf{r}]$ and parity check matrix whose columns consist of all $2^{\mathbf{r}} - 1$ nonzero binary vectors of length $\mathbf{r}$ .

Now, let $n = 2^r - 1$ , and let $\omega$ be a primitive $n$ -th root of unity over $\mathbf{F}_2$ . Since $o_{11}(2) = r$ , the splitting field of $x^{n} - 1$ is $\mathbf{F}_{2^r}$ , and since $\omega$ has order $n = 2^r - 1$ , it is a primitive field element of $\mathbf{F}_{2^r}$ . As a result, the powers of $\omega$ form all nonzero elements of $\mathbf{F}_{2^r}$ , and so the columns of the matrix

consist of all nonzero binary vectors of length $\mathbf{r}$ . This shows that the Hamming code $\mathfrak{H}_2(\mathbf{r})$ with this particular parity check matrix is the cyclic code whose only zeros consist of a primitive $(2^{\mathbf{r}} - 1)$ -st root of unity $\omega$ and (of necessity) all the other roots of the minimal polynomial of $\omega$ .

THE IDEMPOTENT GENERATOR OF A CYCLIC CODE

We have seen that a complete list of all cyclic codes in $\mathbf{R}_{\mathbf{n}}$ can be obtained from a factorization of $x^{n} - 1$ into monic irreducible factors over $\mathbf{F}_{q}$ . However, factoring $x^{n} - 1$ is not an easy task in general. One way to do this is to first obtain a primitive $n$ -th root of unity, but this requires working in the splitting field $\mathbf{F}_{q^s}$ of $x^{n} - 1$ .
In this subsection, we explore another approach to describing cyclic codes, involving a different type of generating polynomial than the generator polynomial.
Definition A polynomial $\mathbf{e}(\mathbf{x}) \in \mathbb{R}_{\mathbf{n}}$ is said to be idempotent in $\mathbb{R}_{\mathbf{n}}$ if $\mathbf{e}(\mathbf{x}) \equiv \mathbf{e}(\mathbf{x})$ .
For example, the polynomial $x^3 + x^5 + x^6$ is an idempotent in $R_7$ . Since $(x^3 + x^5 + x^6)^2 \equiv x^3 + x^5 + x^6$ .

The polynomial $e(x) = a(x)g(x) \mod (x^n - 1)$ has the following properties. $e(x)$ is the unique identity in $C$ , that is,

\mathbf {p} (\mathbf {x}) \mathbf {e} (\mathbf {x}) \equiv \mathbf {p} (\mathbf {x}) \text {f o r a l l} \mathbf {p} (\mathbf {x}) \in C
$$

We will refer to the polynomial $\mathbf{e}(\mathbf{x})$ of the previous theorem as the generating idempotent of $C$ , and write $C = [\mathbf{e}(\mathbf{x})]$ to denote the fact that $\mathbf{e}(\mathbf{x})$ is this generating idempotent. Theorem 7.4.11 Let $C_1 = [e_1(x)]$ and $C_2 = [e_2(x)]$ be cyclic codes in $R_n$ . Then

list_items: 1) $C_1 \subset C_2$ if and only if $e_1(x)e_2(x) \equiv e_1(x)$ ,

The next theorem describes an interesting relationship between the generator polynomial and the generating idempotent of a cyclic code.

Theorem 7.4.12 Let $C$ be a cyclic code in $R_n$ , with generator polynomial $g(x)$ and generating idempotent $e(x)$ . Then $g(x)$ and $e(x)$ have exactly the same roots, in the splitting field for $x^n - 1$ , from among the $n$ -th roots of unity.

Furthermore, if $f(x)$ is an idempotent in $R_{n}$ that has exactly the same roots as $g(x)$ from among the $n$ -th roots of unity, then $f(x)$ is the generating idempotent of $\langle g(x) \rangle$ .

Proof. Let $\omega$ be a primitive $n$ -th root of unity. Since $\mathbf{e}(\mathbf{x}) \equiv \mathbf{a}(\mathbf{x}) \mathbf{g}(\mathbf{x})$ , we deduce that $\mathbf{g}(\boldsymbol{\omega}^{\dot{\mathbf{i}}}) = 0$ implies $\mathbf{e}(\boldsymbol{\omega}^{\dot{\mathbf{i}}}) = 0$ . On the other hand, if $\mathbf{h}(\mathbf{x})$ is the check polynomial for C, then $\mathbf{g}(\boldsymbol{\omega}^{\dot{\mathbf{i}}}) = 0$ if and only if $\mathbf{h}(\boldsymbol{\omega}^{\dot{\mathbf{i}}}) \neq 0$ . Hence, by (7.4.5), $\mathbf{e}(\boldsymbol{\omega}^{\dot{\mathbf{i}}}) = 0$ implies $\mathbf{h}(\boldsymbol{\omega}^{\dot{\mathbf{i}}}) \neq 0$ , which implies that $\mathbf{g}(\boldsymbol{\omega}^{\dot{\mathbf{i}}}) = 0$ .

For the second part of the theorem, we observe that since every root of $\mathbf{g}(\mathbf{x})$ is a root of $\mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x})$ , and since $\mathbf{g}(\mathbf{x})$ has no multiple roots in any extension, we must have $\mathbf{g}(\mathbf{x}) \mid \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x})$ . Furthermore, since the roots of the check polynomial $\mathbf{h}(\mathbf{x})$ are precisely the non-roots of $\mathbf{g}(\mathbf{x})$ , from among the $n$ -th roots of unity, we see that $\mathbf{h}(\mathbf{x})$ and $\mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x})$ have no common roots in any extension. Hence, they are relatively prime. But if $D$ is the cyclic code in $\mathbb{R}_n$ with generating idempotent $\mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x})$ , then by Theorem 7.4.10, the generator polynomial for $D$ is
and so $\mathbf{D} = \mathbf{C}$ . Thus, $\mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x})$ is the generating idempotent of C.
We also have the following result that relates the generating idempotents of a cyclic code and its dual.

Theorem 7.4.13 Let $\mathbf{C} = [\mathbf{e}(\mathbf{x})]$ be a cyclic code with check polynomial $\mathbf{h}(\mathbf{x})$ . Then the cyclic code $\langle \mathbf{h}(\mathbf{x}) \rangle$ has generating idempotent $1 - \mathbf{e}(\mathbf{x})$ and $\mathbf{C}^{\perp} = \langle \mathbf{h}^{\perp}(\mathbf{x}) \rangle$ has generating idempotent

we see that $1 - \mathrm{e}(\mathbf{x})$ is the identity in $\langle \langle \mathbf{h}(\mathbf{x})\rangle \rangle$ . Similarly, since $\mathbf{h}^{\perp}(\mathbf{x}) = \mathrm{h}_{0}^{-1}\mathbf{x}^{\mathbf{k}}\mathrm{h}(\mathbf{x}^{-1}) \equiv \mathrm{h}_{0}^{-1}\mathbf{x}^{\mathbf{k}}\mathrm{h}(\mathbf{x}^{\mathbf{n} - 1})$ , we have
and so $[1 - \mathrm{e}(\mathbf{x}^{n-1})] \bmod (\mathbf{x}^{n} - 1)$ is the identity in $C^{\perp} = \langle \langle \mathbf{h}^{\perp}(\mathbf{x}) \rangle \rangle$ .
Among all cyclic codes in $\mathbf{R}_{\mathfrak{n}}$ , two special types deserve mention. Suppose that

is the factorization of $x^{n} - 1$ into monic irreducible polynomials over $F_{q}$ . In view of Theorem 7.4.3, the cyclic codes $M_{i} = \langle m_{i}(x) \rangle$ are maximal, since the only cyclic code that properly contains $M_{i}$ is $R_{n}$ itself. Similarly, if we let

then the cyclic codes $\widehat{\mathbf{M}}_{\mathbf{i}} = \langle \langle \widehat{\mathbf{m}}_{\mathbf{i}}(\mathbf{x})\rangle \rangle$ are minimal, since the only cyclic code in $\mathbf{R}_{\mathbf{n}}$ that is properly contained in $\widehat{\mathbf{M}}_{\mathbf{i}}$ is the zero code $\{\mathbf{0}\}$ . Minimal cyclic codes are also called irreducible codes.
As the next result shows, the minimal cyclic codes in $\mathbf{R}_{\mathbf{n}}$ are actually fields.

Theorem 7.4.14 Let $\widehat{\mathbf{M}}_{\mathbf{i}} = \langle \langle \widehat{\mathbf{m}}_{\mathbf{i}}(\mathbf{x})\rangle \rangle$ be a minimal cyclic code in $\mathbb{R}_{\mathfrak{n}}$ , where $\deg (\mathfrak{m}_i(\mathbf{x})) = d$ . Then $\widehat{\mathbf{M}}_{\mathbf{i}}$ is a field isomorphic to $\mathbf{F}_{\mathfrak{o}^d}$ .

Proof. Let $f(x)$ be a nonzero polynomial in $\widehat{M}_i$ . Then the ideal $\langle f(x) \rangle$ is contained in the minimal $\widehat{M}_i$ , and so we must have $\widehat{M}_i = \langle f(x) \rangle$ . In particular, if $e(x)$ is the idempotent generator for $\widehat{M}_i$ , then $e(x) \equiv a(x)f(x)$ , for some polynomial $a(x) \in R_n$ . If $a(x) \in \widehat{M}_i$ , it is the inverse of $f(x)$ . In any case, since $e(x) \equiv e^2(x) \equiv a(x)e(x)f(x)$ , and since $a(x)e(x) \bmod (x^n - 1)$ is in $\widehat{M}_i$ , we see that $a(x)e(x) \bmod (x^n - 1)$ is the inverse of $f(x)$ . Hence, $\widehat{M}_i$ is a field. Finally, since $\deg(\widehat{m}_i(x)) = n - d$ , we have $dim(\widehat{M}_i) = d$ over $F_q$ .

It is evident that the generator polynomial of a cyclic code can be obtained, by multiplication, from the generator polynomials of the maximal cyclic codes $\mathbf{M}_{\mathbf{i}} = \langle \mathbf{m}_{\mathbf{i}}(\mathbf{x})\rangle$ . Similarly, the generating idempotent of a cyclic code can be obtained, by addition, from the generating idempotents for the minimal codes $\widehat{\mathbf{M}}_{\mathbf{i}} = \langle \widehat{\mathbf{m}}_{\mathbf{i}}(\mathbf{x})\rangle$ .

Definition The generating idempotent of a minimal cyclic code $\widehat{\mathbf{M}}_{\mathbf{i}} = \langle \widehat{\mathbf{m}}_{\mathbf{j}}(\mathbf{x}) \rangle$ is called a primitive idempotent and is denoted by $\theta_{\mathbf{j}}(\mathbf{x})$ .
Theorem 7.4.15 Let $\theta_{1}(\mathbf{x}),\ldots ,\theta_{s}(\mathbf{x})$ be the primitive idempotents in $\mathbf{R}_{\mathfrak{n}}$ . Then

There are two rather straightforward ways to encode message strings using a cyclic code—one systematic method and one nonsystematic method. We consider the nonsystematic method first. Let $\mathbf{C} = \langle \mathbf{g}(\mathbf{x}) \rangle$ be a q-ary cyclic $[\mathbf{n}, \mathbf{n} - \mathbf{r}]$ -code, where $\deg(\mathbf{g}(\mathbf{x})) = \mathbf{r}$ . Thus, $\mathbf{C}$ is capable of encoding q-ary messages of length $\mathbf{n} - \mathbf{r}$ and requires r redundancy symbols.
Given a source string $\mathbf{a}_0\mathbf{a}_1\dots \mathbf{a}_{\mathbf{n} - \mathbf{r} - 1}$ , we form the message polynomial
This polynomial is encoded as the product $\mathbf{c}(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{a}(\mathbf{x})\mathbf{g}(\mathbf{x}) \in \mathbf{C}$ .
To obtain a systematic encoder, we form the message polynomial
Notice that $\overline{\mathbf{a}} (\mathbf{x})$ has no terms of degree less than $\mathbf{r}$ . Next, we divide $\overline{\mathbf{a}} (\mathbf{x})$ by $\mathbf{g}(\mathbf{x})$ ,

$$
\overline {{a}} (x) = q (x) g (x) + r (x), \text {w h e r e} \deg (r (x)) <   r
$$

and send the codeword $\mathbf{c}(\mathbf{x}) = \overline{\mathbf{a}}(\mathbf{x}) - \mathbf{r}(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{q}(\mathbf{x})\mathbf{g}(\mathbf{x})$ . Since $\overline{\mathbf{a}}(\mathbf{x})$ and $\mathbf{r}(\mathbf{x})$ have no terms of the same degree, this encoder is systematic. In fact, reading the terms of a polynomial from highest degree to lowest degree, we see that the first $\mathbf{n} - \mathbf{r}$ positions are information symbols and the remaining $\mathbf{r}$ positions are check symbols.

Since a cyclic code is a linear code, we can decode using the polynomial form of syndrome decoding. Let $\mathbf{C}$ be a cyclic code. If $\mathbf{c}(\mathbf{x}) \in \mathbf{C}$ is the codeword sent and $\mathbf{u}(\mathbf{x})$ is the received polynomial, then $\text{err}(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{u}(\mathbf{x}) - \mathbf{c}(\mathbf{x})$ is the error polynomial. The weight of a polynomial is the number of nonzero coefficients.

Definition Let $\mathbf{C} = \langle \mathbf{g}(\mathbf{x}) \rangle$ be a cyclic $[\mathbf{n}, \mathbf{n} - \mathbf{r}]$ -code. The syndrome of a polynomial $\mathbf{u}(\mathbf{x})$ , denoted by $\operatorname{syn}(\mathbf{u}(\mathbf{x}))$ , is the remainder upon dividing $\mathbf{u}(\mathbf{x})$ by $\mathbf{g}(\mathbf{x})$ , that is,
In the exercises, we ask the reader to show that this definition of syndrome coincides with the definition of syndrome given for linear codes in Chapter 5.

As expected, a received polynomial $\mathbf{u}(\mathbf{x})$ is a codeword if and only if its syndrome is the zero polynomial. Also, two polynomials have the same syndrome if and only if they lie in the same coset of C. Thus, the polynomial form of syndrome decoding is analogous to the vector form. Let us continue Example 7.5.1.

The main practical difficulty with syndrome decoding is that coset leader-syndrome decoding tables become quite long. However, we can take advantage of the fact that the code in question is cyclic as follows.

Suppose we have a method for decoding the leading coefficient of any received word, which for the purposes of this discussion is the coefficient of $x^{n-1}$ , regardless of whether or not that coefficient is nonzero. Then, if $u(x)$ is received, we can decode its leading coefficient, perform a cyclic shift modulo $x^{n-1}$ , and decode the new leading coefficient, which is the coefficient of $x^{n-2}$ in $u(x)$ . Repeating this process will decode the entire received word. The reason that this method may save time is that we only need those rows of the coset leader-syndrome table that contain coset leaders of degree $n-1$ .
Example 7.5.3 Referring to Example 7.5.2, the only coset leader of weight 1 and degree $n - 1 = 6$ is $x^6$ , and so we need only a one-row table

As in Example 7.5.2, suppose we receive $\mathbf{u}(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{x}^6 + \mathbf{x} + 1$ . Since $\operatorname{syn}(\mathbf{u}(\mathbf{x})) = \mathbf{x}^2 + \mathbf{x}$ is not in the table, we assume that the leading coefficient of $\mathbf{u}(\mathbf{x})$ is correct.

and compute its syndrome, which is just $x^2 + x + 1$ . Again this is not in the table, and so the coefficient of $x^5$ in $u(x)$ is assumed to be correct. Shifting again and computing the syndrome gives
Since the syndrome $x^2 + 1$ is in the table, we deduce that the coefficient of $x^4$ in $u(x)$ is incorrect. Continuing in this manner, we decode $u(x)$ as $x^6 + x^4 + x + 1$ , just as before.

The encoding and decoding of cyclic codes can be achieved using linear switching circuits, which can perform polynomial multiplication and division over finite fields quite efficiently. We will not discuss such circuits in this book, but refer the interested reader to Peterson and Weldon (1972). Suffice it to say that the following theorem, whose proof we leave as an exercise, can sometimes be used to advantage when implementing syndrome decoding using linear circuits.

Theorem 7.5.6 (Meggitt (1960)) Let $\mathbf{C} = \langle \mathbf{g}(\mathbf{x}) \rangle$ be a cyclic code in $\mathbf{R}_{\mathbf{n}}$ . For any polynomial $\mathbf{u}(\mathbf{x}) \in \mathbf{R}_{\mathbf{n}}$ ,

$$
\operatorname {s y n} [ \mathrm {x u} (\mathrm {x}) \bmod (\mathrm {x} ^ {\mathrm {n}} - 1) ] = \operatorname {s y n} (\mathrm {x s y n} (\mathrm {u} (\mathrm {x})))
$$
In words, the cyclic shift of $\mathbf{u}(\mathbf{x})$ , taken modulo $\mathbf{x}^{n-1}$ , and the cyclic shift of the syndrome of $\mathbf{u}(\mathbf{x})$ both have the same syndrome. $\square$
Let us take a closer look at the relationship between the unknown error polynomial $err(\mathbf{x})$ , defined by
where $\mathbf{c}(\mathbf{x})$ is the codeword sent, and the known syndrome $\operatorname{syn}(\mathbf{u}(\mathbf{x}))$ , defined by
Of course, these polynomials need not be the same and, in fact, often have different degrees. However, we can say the following.

Lemma 7.5.7 Let $C$ be a t-error-correcting cyclic code, and suppose that at most $t$ errors have occurred in the transmission of a codeword $c(x)$ . If the syndrome of the received word $u(x)$ has weight at most $t$ , then $err(x) = syn(u(x))$ .
Proof. If $w(\operatorname{syn}(\mathbf{u}(\mathbf{x}))) \leq \mathbf{t}$ , then since $w(err(\mathbf{x})) \leq \mathbf{t}$ , the codeword
has weight at most 2t and so must be the zero codeword. Hence, $err(\mathbf{x}) = \text{syn}(\mathbf{u}(\mathbf{x}))$ .

Of course, we may not be lucky enough to encounter syndromes of weight at most t. However, if the syndrome of a cyclic shift of $\mathbf{u}(\mathbf{x})$ has weight at most t, then it is almost as easy to obtain the error polynomial from this syndrome.

Let us denote the polynomial obtained from $\mathbf{p}(\mathbf{x})$ by performing $\mathbf{k}$ cyclic shifts by $\mathbf{p}^{(\mathbf{k})}(\mathbf{x})$ . Suppose that the syndrome of the cyclic shift $\mathbf{u}^{(\mathbf{k})}(\mathbf{x})$ of $\mathbf{u}(\mathbf{x})$ has weight at most $\mathbf{t}$ . Then since
and so $err(x)$ can be easily recovered from $\mathbf{syn}(\mathbf{u}^{(k)}(\mathbf{x}))$ by shifting an additional $n - k$ places.

Thus, we may define a decoding strategy as follows. Perform successive cyclic shifting of the received word $\mathbf{u}(\mathbf{x})$ until a word $\mathbf{u}^{(\mathbf{k})}(\mathbf{x})$ is encountered, whose syndrome $\mathbf{s}(\mathbf{x}) = \operatorname{syn}(\mathbf{u}^{(\mathbf{k})}(\mathbf{x}))$ has weight at most $t$ . Then
This strategy is known as error trapping.

The next result says that error trapping can correct any t errors that happen to fall within r consecutive positions, including wrap around. (It does not say that any burst of length r or less can be corrected, and we know that this is not possible from Theorem 5.1.17.)

Theorem 7.5.8 Let $C$ be a t-error-correcting cyclic $[n, n-r]$ -code. If $t$ or fewer errors occur, and if they are confined to $r$ consecutive positions (including wrap-around), then the syndrome of some cyclic shift $u^{(k)}(x)$ of the received polynomial $u(x)$ has weight at most $t$ .

Proof. There must exist some $\mathbf{k}$ for which the cyclic shift $\mathbf{u}^{(\mathbf{k})}(\mathbf{x})$ of $\mathbf{u}(\mathbf{x})$ has its errors confined to the $\mathbf{r}$ coefficients of $\mathbf{x}^0, \ldots, \mathbf{x}^{r-1}$ . Thus,

and so the syndrome of $\mathbf{u}^{(\mathbf{k})}(\mathbf{x})$ has weight at most t.

Definition A burst of length $\mathbf{b}$ in $\mathbf{R}_{\mathbf{n}}$ is a polynomial in $\mathbf{R}_{\mathbf{n}}$ whose nonzero coefficients are confined to $\mathbf{b}$ consecutive positions, allowing for wrap-around, the first and last of which must be nonzero.

Theorem 7.5.9 A cyclic $[n, n - r]$ -code $C$ contains no bursts of length $r$ or less. Hence, it can detect any burst error of length $r$ or less. $\square$

According to Theorem 5.1.17, if a cyclic $[\mathbf{n},\mathbf{n} - \mathbf{r}]$ -code $C$ can correct all burst errors of length $b$ or less (not including wrap around), then we must have $2b \leq r$ . Hence, the degree $r$ of the generator polynomial $g(x)$ of $C$ provides an upper bound on the quantity $2b$ . A bound is easily achieved.
As we will see, this upper bound is easily achieved.

Then we may interleave these codewords by juxtaposing the first

(The parentheses are included to aid readability only.)

Let us denote by $\mathbf{C}^{(\mathbf{k})}$ the set of all words formed in this way from all possible choices of $\mathbf{k}$ codewords $\mathbf{c}_0, \ldots, \mathbf{c}_{\mathbf{k}-1}$ (taken in all possible orders). The code $\mathbf{C}^{(\mathbf{k})}$ is said to be formed from $\mathbf{C}$ by interleaving (or symbol interleaving), and $\mathbf{k}$ is called the interleaving degree. The key properties of $\mathbf{C}^{(\mathbf{k})}$ are given in the next theorem, whose proof is left for the reader.

Theorem 7.5.10 Let $\mathbf{C} = \langle \langle \mathbf{g}(\mathbf{x}) \rangle \rangle$ be a cyclic [n,s]-code. Then the code $\mathbf{C}^{(\mathbf{k})}$ , obtained from $\mathbf{C}$ by symbol interleaving, is a cyclic [nk,sk]-code with generator polynomial $\mathbf{g}(\mathbf{x}^{\mathbf{k}})$ . Furthermore, if $\mathbf{C}$ is capable of correcting burst errors of length b or less, then $\mathbf{C}^{(\mathbf{k})}$ is capable of correcting burst errors of length bk or less. $\square$

Theorem 7.5.10 tells us that if we can find short cyclic codes with good burst error correcting capabilities, then through the process of symbol interleaving, we can also find long cyclic codes with proportionally good burst error correcting capabilities.

Computer searches have been done to find short cyclic codes with good burst error correcting capabilities, to which interleaving can apply. For a list of such codes, see Peterson and Weldon (1972). Here is one example.

Example 7.5.4 The binary cyclic [7,3]-code $C = \langle \mathbf{x}^4 + \mathbf{x}^3 + \mathbf{x}^2 + 1 \rangle$ is capable of correcting burst errors of length 2 or less, which is the best possible since, in this case, $r = 2b$ . Hence, according to Theorem 7.5.10, the interleaved code $C^{(k)} = \langle \mathbf{x}^{4k} + \mathbf{x}^{3k} + \mathbf{x}^{2k} + 1 \rangle$ is a binary cyclic [7k,3k]-code that can correct burst errors of length 2k or less. Note that this is again best possible.

This theorem tells us that we can obtain a code with minimum distance at least a prescribed amount $\delta$ by specifying that its generator polynomial have $\delta - 1$ "consecutive" zeros. (That is, the exponents are consecutive.) Theorem 8.1.1 can be generalized as follows. (We leave the proof as an exercise.)

among its zeros, where $\mathbf{r}$ and $\mathbf{n}$ are relatively prime and $\mathbf{b} \geq 0$ . Then $\mathbf{C}$ has minimum distance at least $\delta$ .

We are now ready to define the BCH codes. These codes were discovered independently by R.C. Bose and D.K. Ray-Chaudhuri (1960) and by A. Hocquenghem (1959). They form an extremely important class of codes for several reasons. For instance, they have good error-correcting properties when the length is not too great, they can be encoded and decoded relatively easily, and they provide a good foundation upon which to base other families of codes.

Definition Let $\omega$ be a primitive $n$ -th root of unity over $\mathbf{F}_{q}$ , and let $\mathbf{g}(\mathbf{x})$ be the monic polynomial over $\mathbf{F}_{q}$ of smallest degree that has the $\delta - 1$ numbers
among its zeros, where $\mathbf{b} \geq 0$ and $\delta \geq 1$ . Thus,

The q-ary cyclic code $\mathfrak{B}_{\mathbf{q}}(\mathbf{n},\delta ,\omega ,\mathbf{b})$ of length n, with generator polynomial $\mathbf{g}(\mathbf{x})$ , is called a BCH code with designed distance $\delta$ . (Note that the designed distance is one greater than the number of zeros.)

When $\mathbf{b} = 1$ , the code $\mathfrak{B}_{\mathbf{q}}(\mathbf{n},\delta,\omega) = \mathfrak{B}_{\mathbf{q}}(\mathbf{n},\delta,\omega,1)$ is referred to as a narrow sense BCH code. When $\omega$ is a primitive field element, that is, when $\mathbf{n} = \mathbf{q}^{\mathbf{s}} - 1$ for some $\mathbf{s} \geq 1$ , then $\mathfrak{B}_{\mathbf{q}}(\mathbf{n},\delta,\omega,\mathbf{b})$ is referred to as a primitive BCH code.

The binary narrow sense BCH codes are generalizations of the Hamming codes. In particular, we have seen that the binary Hamming codes can be defined as cyclic codes whose generator polynomial is the monic polynomial of smallest degree over $\mathbf{F}_2$ that has a primitive $n$ -th root of unity $\omega$ as a zero. Hence, these Hamming codes are binary primitive narrow sense BCH codes of designed distance $\delta = 2$ .

According to Theorem 8.1.1, the BCH code $\mathfrak{B}_{\mathbf{q}}(\mathbf{n},\delta ,\omega ,\mathbf{b})$ has minimum distance at least equal to its designed distance $\delta$ . We have already mentioned that BCH codes have good error correcting properties when the length is not too great. In fact, for lengths of a few thousand or less, the BCH codes are among the best codes known. On the other hand, for large lengths, the BCH codes are not good. Recall that, according to Corollary 4.2.7, we cannot expect asymptotically good error correcting properties from a family of codes for which the quantity $\frac{d}{n}$ tends to 0 as $n$ tends to infinity. In this regard, we have the following theorem, whose proof can be found in MacWilliams and Sloane (1977).
Theorem 8.1.11 There does not exist an infinite sequence of q-ary primitive BCH codes with the property that both $\mathrm{d} / \mathrm{n}$ and $\mathbf{k} / \mathbf{n}$ are bounded away from 0.

Then, according to Theorem 8.1.4, the dimension of $\mathfrak{B}$ is at least $n - 2s$ . Example 8.1.4 shows that $\mathfrak{B}$ has minimum distance 5 and so is exactly double-error-correcting. It is interesting to compare this with the single-error-correcting Hamming code $\mathfrak{H}_2(\mathbf{s})$ , which has parameters $[n = 2^s - 1, n - s, 3]$ . Notice that the rates

are quite similar for even modest codeword lengths, and yet the BCH code corrects an additional error. We also mention that these BCH codes, while not perfect like the Hamming codes, are the next best thing - they are quasi-perfect. (See Section 4.2.)

Theorem 8.1.12 For $n \geq 15$ , let $\mathfrak{B}_2(n,5,\omega)$ be a double-error-correcting binary primitive narrow sense BCH code with designed distance 5. Assume that at most two errors have occurred in receiving the word $u(x)$ , and let $u(\omega) = u_1$ and $u(\omega^3) = u_3$ .

3) If $u_1 \neq 0$ and $u_3 \neq u_1^3$ , then two errors have occurred. Furthermore, the locator polynomial

text: In this section, we discuss two special types of BCH codes, beginning with the very useful Reed-Solomon codes.

Reed-Solomon codes are used quite often in practice. In fact, use of these codes, in conjunction with non-block codes, has been a standard procedure at NASA since the launch of the Voyager spacecraft in 1977. These codes have been used, for instance, on the Galileo, Magellan, and Ulysses missions.

As we will see, Reed-Solomon codes are maximum distance separable (MDS) and hence have the largest possible minimum distance for codes of their size and dimension. Also, they are very useful as building blocks for other codes and in correcting burst errors.

Definition Let $q \geq 3$ . A q-ary Reed-Solomon code (or RS code) $\mathcal{F} = \mathcal{F}(n, \delta, \omega, b)$ is a q-ary BCH code $\mathfrak{B}_{\mathbf{q}}(q - 1, \delta, \omega, b)$ with length $n = q - 1$ . $\square$
Thus, the length of a q-ary Reed-Solomon code is equal to the number of nonzero elements in the base field $\mathbf{F}_{\mathbf{q}}$ .
Since $\mathbf{n} = \mathbf{q} - 1$ , and since the roots of the polynomial $x^{q - 1} - 1$ are precisely the nonzero elements of $\mathbf{F}_q$ , we have
Thus, if $\omega$ is a primitive (q-1)-st root of unity over $\mathbf{F}_{\mathbf{q}}$ (that is, a primitive element of $\mathbf{F}_{\mathbf{q}}$ ), then
As a result, a Reed-Solomon code with designed distance $\delta$ has generator polynomial

Example 8.2.1 Let $\mathbf{q} = 8$ , and so $n = 7$ . A root $\omega$ of the primitive polynomial $x^3 + x + 1$ over $\mathbf{F}_2$ gives us a primitive element of $\mathbf{F}_8$ . To obtain an RS code of dimension 5, we want $\deg(\mathbf{g}(\mathbf{x})) = 7 - 5 = 2$ . For instance, we could take
The Reed-Solomon Codes are MDS Codes
The generator polynomial of a Reed-Solomon code of designed distance $\delta$ has degree $\deg(g(x)) = \delta - 1$ . Hence,

$$
k = d i m (\mathcal {P} (n, \delta , \omega , b)) = n - \deg (g (x)) = n - \delta + 1
$$
and so, according to the BCH bound, the minimum distance of this code satisfies
But the Singleton bound is $\mathbf{d} \leq \mathbf{n} - \mathbf{k} + 1$ , and so
which tells us that $\wp(n, \delta, \omega, b)$ is an MDS code and also that the minimum distance is equal to the designed distance.
Theorem 8.2.1 Reed-Solomon codes are maximum distance separable (MDS) codes. Furthermore, the minimum distance of a Reed-Solomon code is equal to its designed distance. $\square$
Hence, RS codes have the largest possible minimum distance among all q-ary codes of length $n = q - 1$ and dimension $k = n - \delta + 1$ .
The Dual of a Reed-Solomon Code
In general, the dual of a BCH code may not be another BCH code. For instance, let $\mathbf{n} = 25$ and $\mathbf{q} = 2$ . The cyclotomic cosets of $\mathbf{q}$ modulo $\mathbf{n}$ are

Let $\mathfrak{B} = \mathfrak{B}_2(25,1,\omega)$ be the BCH code with defining zero $\omega$ . The dual code $\mathfrak{B}^{\perp}$ has zeros that are the reciprocals of the nonzeros of $\mathfrak{B}$ , and so $\mathfrak{B}^{\perp}$ has zeros
Hence, $\mathfrak{B}^{\perp}$ is not a BCH code. On the other hand, for Reed-Solomon codes, we have the following, whose proof is left as an exercise.
Theorem 8.2.2 The dual of a Reed-Solomon code is a Reed-Solomon code. $\square$

Recall from Section 4.3 that if $C$ is a q-ary $(n, M, d)$ -code, the extended code $\widehat{C}$ is defined by adding an overall parity check $c_n$ to each codeword $c = c_0 \cdots c_{n-1}$ that satisfies

In the exercises, we ask the reader to provide an example of a code $C_1$ for which $\widehat{d}_1 = d_1$ and a code $C_2$ for which $\widehat{d}_2 = d_2 + 1$ . On the other hand, for Reed-Solomon codes, the minimum distance of the extended code is always larger than the minimum distance of the original.
Theorem 8.2.3 Let $\mathcal{F}$ be an $[\mathbf{n},\mathbf{k},\mathbf{d}]$ Reed-Solomon code with generator polynomial
Then the extended code $\widehat{\mathcal{P}}$ is an $[\mathbf{n} + 1,\mathbf{k},\mathbf{d} + 1]$ -code.
Proof. Let $\mathbf{c}(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{c}_0 + \mathbf{c}_1\mathbf{x} + \dots + \mathbf{c}_{n-1}\mathbf{x}^{n-1}$ be a codeword in $\wp$ of weight $d$ . Extending $\mathbf{c}(\mathbf{x})$ gives
We want to show that the weight of $\widehat{\mathbf{c}}(\mathbf{x})$ is $d + 1$ , which is true if and only if $\mathbf{c}(1) \neq 0$ .

Since $\mathbf{g}(\mathbf{x})$ is the generator polynomial for $\wp$ , we have $\mathbf{c}(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{p}(\mathbf{x})\mathbf{g}(\mathbf{x})$ for some polynomial $\mathbf{p}(\mathbf{x})$ , and so $\mathbf{c}(1) = \mathbf{p}(1)\mathbf{g}(1)$ . Clearly, $\mathbf{g}(1) \neq 0$ , since $\omega^i \neq 1$ for $i = 1,\ldots ,d - 1$ . If $\mathbf{p}(1) = 0$ , then the polynomial $\mathbf{g}_1(\mathbf{x}) = (\mathbf{x} - 1)\mathbf{g}(\mathbf{x})$ would divide $\mathbf{c}(\mathbf{x})$ . Hence, $\mathbf{c}(\mathbf{x})$ would be in the cyclic code generated by

which has d "consecutive" zeros $\omega^0,\ldots ,\omega^{d - 1}$ , and so by the BCH bound, $\mathbf{c}(\mathbf{x})$ would have weight at least $\mathbf{d} + 1$ . This is a contradiction to the fact that $\mathbf{c}(\mathbf{x})$ has weight d. Hence, $\mathfrak{p}(1)\neq 0$ and the proof is complete.
OBTAINING A BINARY CODE FROM A $2^{\mathbf{m}}$ -ARY CODE

In other words, the weight of each $(\mathbf{m} + 1)$ -string is at least 2, and so the total weight of a codeword in $\mathbf{C}^{\uparrow}$ is at least 2d. Since Reed-Solomon codes are BCH codes, they can be decoded by the method described in the previous section. Also, we will discuss another, more efficient, decoding procedure in Section 8.3. However, let us now briefly discuss the original method of Reed and Solomon, which is based on a form of majority logic. Unfortunately, this method, while interesting, is not very practical for $\mathbf{\{n,k,d\}}$ -codes in which $\binom{n}{k}$ is large.
As in the previous subsection, suppose that the original message is
is transmitted. In string notation, the message is
Now, let $\mathbf{u} = \mathbf{u}_0 \cdots \mathbf{u}_{n-1}$ be the received word and $\mathbf{e} = \mathbf{e}_0 \cdots \mathbf{e}_{n-1}$ be the error. Thus, the quantities

If there are no errors in transmission, then the $\mathbf{e}_i$ 's are all 0, and any $k$ of these equations can be solved for the $k$ unknowns $a_0, \ldots, a_{k-1}$ . This follows from the fact that the matrix of coefficients of any $k$ equations is a Vandermonde matrix. (See Lemma 5.3.14.)

On the other hand, suppose that $t$ errors have occurred. Let us refer to the equations in (8.2.4) for which $e_i = 0$ as good equations and the equations for which $e_i \neq 0$ as the bad equations. Hence, there are $t$ bad equations and $n - t$ good equations.

If we solve all subsystems of $\mathbf{k}$ equations in (8.2.4), then the $(\mathbf{\Pi}_{\mathbf{k}}^{\mathbf{n} - \mathbf{t}})$ subsystems consisting of all good equations will give the correct values of the $\mathbf{a}_{\mathbf{i}}$ 's. Moreover, a given incorrect solution cannot satisfy any set of $\mathbf{k}$ good equations, and so it is a solution to at most $\mathbf{t} + \mathbf{k} - 1$ equations, that is, it can be obtained at most $(\mathbf{\Pi}_{\mathbf{k}}^{\mathbf{t} + \mathbf{k} - 1})$ times from subsystems. Therefore, if
then the majority solution from among the $\binom{n}{k}$ solutions will give the true values of the $a_{j}$ 's.
Finally, we observe that (8.2.5) holds if and only if $n - t > t + k - 1$ , that is, if and only if $d = n - k + 1 > 2t$ .

Theorem 8.2.6 Let $\mathfrak{F}$ be an $[\mathbf{n},\mathbf{k},\mathbf{d}]$ Reed-Solomon code. The majority logic decoding method of this subsection will correct up to $t < \frac{1}{2}$ d errors in transmission, at the cost of having to solve $\binom{\mathbf{n}}{\mathbf{k}}$ systems of equations of size $\mathbf{k} \times \mathbf{k}$ . $\square$

We remarked in the previous section that BCH codes do not have good error correcting capabilities for large codeword lengths. More precisely, Theorem 8.1.11 says that there does not exist an infinite sequence of q-ary primitive BCH codes with the property that both $\mathbf{d} / \mathbf{n}$ and $\mathbf{k} / \mathbf{n}$ are bounded away from 0.

A family $\mathcal{F}$ of q-ary codes is said to be asymptotically good if $\mathcal{F}$ contains an infinite sequence of codes $C_1, C_2, \ldots$ , where $C_i$ has parameters $[n_i, k_i, d_i]$ , with the property that the rates $R(C_i) = k_i / n_i$ and the numbers $d_i / n_i$ are bounded away from 0. Otherwise, the family $\mathcal{F}$ is asymptotically bad. As we saw in Corollary 4.2.7, asymptotically bad families of codes either have their rates tending to 0 or their probabilities of error correction tending to 0.

Thus, BCH codes form an asymptotically bad family of codes. It can also be shown that the binary codes $\mathfrak{F}^{\rightarrow}$ that come from Reed-Solomon codes, as described earlier in this section, are asymptotically bad.

There is an elegantly simple way to construct a good family of codes - at least in theory. Let $0 \neq \alpha \in \mathbf{F}_{2^m}$ . Thinking of the elements of $\mathbf{F}_{2^m}$ as m-tuples in $\mathbf{F}_{2^m}$ as well, let
This is a binary code of length $n = 2m$ , dimension $k = m$ , and hence rate $R = 1/2$ .

Finally, since $\lambda_{\mathbf{m}} \rightarrow \mathrm{H}^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)$ as $\mathbf{m} \rightarrow \infty$ , we have found a sequence of codes $C_{\beta_{\mathbf{m}}}$ of length $\mathbf{n} = 2\mathbf{m}$ , rate $\mathbf{R} = 1/2$ , and

Unfortunately, as is often the case, the practice has not kept up with the theory, and we do not yet know how to choose the appropriate $\beta_{\mathbf{m}}$ 's to obtain a concrete example of a good family of codes in this way. However, in 1972, Justesen devised a clever method to avoid having to find appropriate $\beta_{\mathbf{m}}$ 's. His idea was to use all nonzero elements of $\mathbf{F}_{2^{\mathbf{m}}}$ and employ a construction known as concatenation of codes.

Concatenation of codes is a construction that is used quite often in practice. For example, NASA often uses a code constructed by concatenating a Reed-Solomon code with a convolutional (nonblock) code.

Consider a binary $\mathbf{k}_1\mathbf{k}_2$ -tuple $\mathbf{a}$ . Referring to Figure 8.2.2, we group $\mathbf{a}$ into $\mathbf{k}_1$ individual $\mathbf{k}_2$ -tuples. Each binary $\mathbf{k}_2$ -tuple can be thought of as an element of the field $\mathrm{GF}(2^{\mathbf{k}_2})$ , and so $\mathbf{a}$ can be thought of as a $\mathbf{k}_1$ -tuple
Now let $C_1$ be an $[n_1, k_1, d_1]$ -code over $GF(2^{k_2})$ . We refer to $C_1$ as the outer code. The string $a$ is encoded, using the outer code $C_1$ , into an $n_1$ -tuple

also over $\mathrm{GF}(2^{\mathbf{k}_2})$ . Next, each one of the $\mathfrak{n}_1$ individual $2^{\mathbf{k}_2}$ -tuples is encoded, using an $[\mathfrak{n}_2,\mathfrak{k}_2,\mathfrak{d}_2]$ -code $\mathbf{C}_2$ , known as the inner code. The
result is a string of length $\mathfrak{n}_1\mathfrak{n}_2$

Thus, the two-step encoding process, referred to as superencoding, takes a $\mathbf{k}_1\mathbf{k}_2$ -tuple as input and produces an $\mathfrak{n}_1\mathfrak{n}_2$ -tuple as output. The resulting code is referred to as a supercode and has length $\mathfrak{n}_1\mathfrak{n}_2$ and dimension $\mathbf{k}_1\mathbf{k}_2$ . We leave it as an exercise to show that the minimum distance of the supercode is at least $\mathbf{d}_1\mathbf{d}_2$ .

Now we can describe the construction of Justesen codes. We begin by observing that the inner code in Figure 8.2.2 is used to encode each of the strings $\mathbf{c}_i$ separately, and so there is no reason why different inner codes cannot be used for different $\mathbf{c}_i$ 's.
Let the outer code $\mathbf{C}_1$ be an $[n = 2^{m} - 1, k, d]$ Reed-Solomon code $\wp$ over $\mathbf{F}_{2^m}$ . Any binary $(k2^{m})$ -tuple $a$ of message symbols can be written in the form
Encoding a using $\wp$ gives a codeword

discussed earlier. Encoding $\mathbf{c}$ using these inner codes gives the supercodeword
where the parentheses and commas are included for readability.

The Justesen code $\mathfrak{g}_{\mathbf{n},\mathbf{k}}$ is the set of all binary words $\mathbf{d}$ obtained in this way from the $[\mathbf{n},\mathbf{k},\mathbf{d}]$ -Reed-Solomon code $\mathfrak{g}$ , where each symbol in $\mathbf{d}$ is interpreted as a binary (2m)-tuple. The Justesen code $\mathfrak{g}_{\mathbf{n},\mathbf{k}}$ has parameters
AN ASYMPTOTICALLY GOOD FAMILY OF JUSTESEN CODES

Recall that our goal in constructing the Justesen codes is to obtain a sequence of codes $\mathfrak{g}_{\mathfrak{n},\mathbf{k}}$ for which $\mathbf{k} / \mathbf{n}$ and $\mathbf{d} / \mathbf{n}$ are bounded away from 0 as $\mathfrak{n}\to \infty$ .
Let us fix $0 < \mathbf{R}_0 < 1/2$ and choose a sequence of outer RS codes $\wp_{\mathfrak{n}}$ with parameters
Hence, the rates of the corresponding Justesen codes $\mathfrak{J}_{\mathbf{n},\mathbf{k}}$ are
This takes care of one-half of our goal.

Now we must consider the minimum weights $w(\mathfrak{g}_{\mathbf{n}, \mathbf{k}})$ . Fix a value of $\mathbf{n}$ , and consider the code $\mathfrak{g}_{\mathbf{n}, \mathbf{k}}$ . If the outer Reed-Solomon code $\mathfrak{g}_{\mathbf{n}}$ has minimum weight $\mathbf{d}$ , any supercodeword
in $\mathfrak{g}_{\mathbf{n},\mathbf{k}}$ will have at least d nonzero first components $c_0,\dots ,c_{n - 1}$ Furthermore, recalling an earlier discussion, we know that any two of the inner codes
have only the zero string in common, and so the corresponding d
ordered pairs, being in different inner codes, must be distinct. In other words, $\mathbf{d}$ has at least $d$ distinct nonzero binary (2m)-tuples.

Since the weight of the supercodeword $\mathbf{d} \in \mathcal{J}_{\mathbf{n},\mathbf{k}}$ is at least equal to the sum of the weights of these $d$ distinct nonzero binary (2m)-tuples, we need to determine a lower bound on such a sum. To this end, we observe that

$$
d = n - k + 1 = n \left(1 - \frac {k - 1}{n}\right) \geq n \left(1 - 2 R _ {0}\right) = n S
$$

where $\mathbf{S} = 1 - 2\mathbf{R}_0$ , and so a supercodeword $\mathbf{d}$ in $\mathcal{J}_{\mathbf{n},\mathbf{k}}$ has at least nS distinct nonzero binary (2m)-tuples. The following lemma is just what we need.

Since $\mathbf{m} \to \infty$ if and only if $\mathbf{n} \to \infty$ , the result follows.
Using Lemma 8.2.7, we conclude that the code $\mathfrak{g}_{\mathbf{n},\mathbf{k}}$ has minimum weight at least
Thus, we see that the sequence of Justesen codes $\mathfrak{J}_{\mathbf{n},\mathbf{k}}$ , based on Reed-Solomon codes $\mathfrak{F}_{\mathbf{n}}$ with parameters

$$
R \left(j _ {n, k}\right) \geq R _ {0} > 0 \text {a n d} \frac {w \left(j _ {n , k}\right)}{\operatorname {l e n} \left(j _ {n , k}\right)} \rightarrow c (1 - 2 R _ {0}) > 0 \quad (c \approx 0. 1)
$$
and is therefore an asymptotically good family of codes.

The family of Justesen codes $\mathfrak{g}_{\mathbf{n},\mathbf{k}}$ have rate at most $1/2$ . However, by puncturing the Justesen codes $\mathfrak{g}_{\mathbf{n},\mathbf{k}}$ in appropriate places, it is possible to obtain an asymptotically good family of codes whose rate approaches any number $\mathbf{R}_0$ with $0 < \mathbf{R}_0 < 1$ . The details of this procedure can be found in MacWilliams and Sloane (1977).

list_items: 1. Find the generator polynomial of an RS code of length 15 and dimension 11. What is the minimum distance of this code.
2. Find the generator polynomial of a [10,6] Reed-Solomon code. What is the minimum distance of this code?
3. Find a double-error-correcting Reed-Solomon code over $\mathbf{F}_{16}$ . What is the length and the dimension of this code?
4. Show that the code $\mathbf{C}^{\perp}$ whose zeros are given by (8.2.1) is not a BCH code.
5. Prove that the dual of a Reed-Solomon code is also a Reed-Solomon code.
6. Find a code $C_1$ for which $\widehat{d}_1 = d_1$ and a code $C_2$ for which $\widehat{d}_2 = d_2 + 1$ .
7. Verify the list of codewords for $\pmb{\varphi}$ in Example 8.2.2.
8. Using the codewords $\mathfrak{F}$ in Example 8.2.2, compute the extended code $\widehat{\mathfrak{F}}$ .
9. Referring to Example 8.2.2, compute the codewords in $\pmb{\varphi}^{\dagger}$
10. Verify that, if a $2^{\mathfrak{m}}$ -ary code $\mathbf{C}$ is t-error-correcting, then the expanded version $\mathbf{C} \rightarrow$ can correct bursts of length up to $(t - 1)\mathbf{m} + 1$ .
11. Design a code, based on a Reed-Solomon code, that can correct burst errors of length up to 25.

12. Referring to the subsection on idempotents, show that each codeword in the minimal code $\widehat{\mathbf{M}}_j$ has weight $n$ . Hint. Show that $\mathbf{g}_j(x)$ has weight $n$ . Start with the constant term.
13. Show that the primitive idempotent $\theta_{\mathbf{j}}(\mathbf{x})$ given by (8.2.2) really does have constant terms equal to 1.
14. What do the Mattson-Solomon polynomials have to do with the encoding of RS codes as described in this section?

15. Show that the minimum distance of a supercode formed from an $\left[\mathbf{n}_{1},\mathbf{k}_{1},\mathbf{d}_{1}\right]$ outer code and an $\left[\mathbf{n}_{2},\mathbf{k}_{2},\mathbf{d}_{2}\right]$ outer code is at least $\mathbf{d}_{1}\mathbf{d}_{2}$ .

16. Let $\mathbf{a}$ be a binary $\mathbf{k}_1\mathbf{k}_2$ -tuple. Suppose we group $\mathbf{a}$ into $\mathbf{k}_2$ individual $\mathbf{k}_1$ -tuples and encode each $\mathbf{k}_1$ -tuple using an $[\mathbf{n}_1,\mathbf{k}_1,\mathbf{d}_1]$ -code $\mathbf{C}_1$ , resulting in a string of $\mathbf{k}_2$ individual $\mathbf{n}_1$ -tuples. Then we encode this string using an $[\mathbf{n}_2,\mathbf{k}_2,\mathbf{d}_2]$ -code over $\mathrm{GF}(2^{\mathbf{n}_1})$ . How does this construction differ from concatenation? What can you say about the minimum distance of this type of "super" code?

17. Let the inner code be the $[7,4,3]$ Hamming code $\mathfrak{H}_2(3)$ , and let the outer code be the $[15,13,7]$ Reed-Solomon code C. Describe the superencoding procedure, using the nonsystematic encoding procedure of Section 7.5. Then perform the encoding on the 52-tuple

text: We pause in our study of cyclic codes to discuss a generalization of BCH codes known as alternant codes, which include several important families of codes. While these codes are, in general, not cyclic, they are very powerful codes. This is exemplified by the fact that, unlike BCH codes, they do form an asymptotically good family in the sense of Section 8.2.
We begin with a few remarks on matrices. Let

be an $\mathbf{r} \times \mathbf{n}$ matrix over the field $\mathbf{F}_{\mathbf{q}^{\mathbf{m}}}$ . Thinking of the elements $\mathbf{c}$ in $\mathbf{F}_{\mathbf{q}^{\mathbf{m}}}$ as column vectors [c] in $\mathbf{F}_{\mathbf{q}}^{\mathbf{m}}$ , we can write

where $\mathbf{M}'$ is an $\mathbf{mr} \times \mathbf{n}$ matrix with entries in the base field $\mathbf{F}_{\mathbf{q}}$ . If $\mathbf{a}_{\mathbf{j}} \in \mathbf{F}_{\mathbf{q}^{\mathbf{m}}}$ , then of course

$$
\left[ \begin{array}{l l l} c _ {1 1} & \dots & c _ {1 n} \\ \vdots & \dots & \vdots \\ c _ {r 1} & \dots & c _ {r m} \end{array} \right] \left[ \begin{array}{l} a _ {1} \\ \vdots \\ a _ {n} \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array}{c} a _ {1} c _ {1 1} + a _ {2} c _ {1 2} + \dots + a _ {n} c _ {1 n} \\ \vdots \\ a _ {1} c _ {r 1} + a _ {2} c _ {r 2} + \dots + a _ {n} c _ {r m} \end{array} \right]
$$
Furthermore, provided that a lies in the base field $\mathbf{F}_{\mathbf{q}}$ , we have

$$
\left[ \begin{array}{c} \left[ c _ {1 1} \right] \dots \left[ c _ {1 n} \right] \\ \vdots \dots \vdots \\ \left[ c _ {r 1} \right] \dots \left[ c _ {r n} \right] \end{array} \right] \left[ \begin{array}{l} a _ {1} \\ \vdots \\ a _ {n} \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array}{c} \left[ a _ {1} c _ {1 1} + a _ {2} c _ {1 2} + \dots + a _ {n} c _ {1 n} \right] \\ \vdots \\ \left[ a _ {1} c _ {r 1} + a _ {2} c _ {r 2} + \dots + a _ {n} c _ {r n} \right] \end{array} \right]
$$

It follows that if the columns of $\mathbf{M}$ are linearly independent as r-vectors over $\mathbf{F}_{\mathbf{q}^{\mathbf{m}}}$ , they are also linearly independent when thought of as vectors over $\mathbf{F}_{\mathbf{q}}^{\mathbf{q}}$ , and so the columns of $\mathbf{M}'$ are linearly independent over $\mathbf{F}_{\mathbf{q}}$ .
Now let us recall from Section 8.1 that if $\omega$ is a primitive $n$ -th root of unity in $\mathbf{F}_{q^m}$ , the matrix
is a parity check matrix for a narrow sense BCH code $\mathfrak{B}$ (once all dependent rows have been purged).
We can generalize this by considering the matrices

where $\mathbf{h}_1, \ldots, \mathbf{h}_n$ are nonzero elements of $\mathbf{F}_{\mathbf{q}^{\mathbf{m}}}$ , and $\alpha_1, \ldots, \alpha_n$ are distinct elements of $\mathbf{F}_{\mathbf{q}^{\mathbf{m}}}$ . Thus, $\mathbf{H}$ is an $\mathbf{r} \times \mathbf{n}$ matrix over $\mathbf{F}_{\mathbf{q}^{\mathbf{m}}}$ , whereas $\mathbf{H}'$ is an $\mathbf{r} \mathbf{m} \times \mathbf{n}$ matrix over $\mathbf{F}_{\mathbf{q}}$ . We will assume that $\mathbf{r} < \mathbf{n}$ .

Observe that any $r$ columns of $H$ forms a square submatrix $K$ that is similar to a Vandermonde matrix. We leave it to the reader to show that any such submatrix has nonzero determinant, and so any $r$ columns of $H$ are linearly independent over $F_{q^m}$ . Therefore, according to our previous remarks, any $r$ columns of $H'$ are linearly independent over $F_q$ .
We can now define alternant codes.

Definition Let $\alpha = (\alpha_{1},\ldots ,\alpha_{n})$ , where the $\alpha_{i}$ 's are distinct elements of $\mathbf{F}_{q^{m}}$ , and let $\mathbf{h} = (h_1,\dots,h_n)$ , where the $\mathbf{h}_{j}$ 's are nonzero elements of $\mathbf{F}_{q^{m}}$ . Let $r < n$ . An alternating code $\mathcal{A}(\alpha ,\mathbf{h})$ is a code over $\mathbf{F}_{q^{\prime}}$ with parity check matrix of the form $\mathbf{H}'$ (deleting any dependent rows). $\square$
Theorem 8.3.1 The alternant code $\mathcal{A}(\alpha, \mathbf{h})$ with parity check matrix $\mathbf{H}'$ (deleting any dependent rows) has length $n$ , dimension $k$ satisfying

Thus, we see that alternant codes are generalizations of BCH codes. Although we will not go into the details, it can be shown that there exist arbitrarily long alternant codes that meet the Gilbert-Varshamov bound (See Section 4.5 on asymptotic results). Hence, the family of alternant codes is asymptotically good.
Example 8.3.1 Let us take $\mathbf{q} = 2$ and $\mathbf{m} = 3$ . If $\omega$ is a primitive element of $\mathbf{F}_8$ , let

$$
\mathbf {H} = \left[ \begin{array}{c c c c c c c} 1 & 1 & 1 & 1 & 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & \omega & \omega^ {2} & \omega^ {3} & \omega^ {4} & \omega^ {5} & \omega^ {6} \end{array} \right]
$$
Using the field table for $\mathbf{F_8}$ , we find that

$$
\mathbf {H} ^ {\prime} = \left[ \begin{array}{c c c c c c c} 1 & 1 & 1 & 1 & 1 & 1 & 1 \\ \mathbf {0} & \mathbf {0} & \mathbf {0} & \mathbf {0} & \mathbf {0} & \mathbf {0} & \mathbf {0} \\ \mathbf {0} & \mathbf {0} & \mathbf {0} & \mathbf {0} & \mathbf {0} & \mathbf {0} & \mathbf {0} \\ 1 & \mathbf {0} & \mathbf {0} & 1 & \mathbf {0} & 1 & 1 \\ \mathbf {0} & 1 & \mathbf {0} & 1 & 1 & 1 & \mathbf {0} \\ \mathbf {0} & \mathbf {0} & 1 & \mathbf {0} & 1 & 1 & 1 \end{array} \right]
$$

Purging the second and third rows of this matrix gives us a parity check matrix $\mathbf{H}''$ of size $4 \times 7$ . Hence the binary alternant code $\mathcal{A}(\alpha, \mathbf{h})$ with parity check matrix $\mathbf{H}''$ has parameters [7,3,4]. Note that, in this case, the minimum distance exceeds the lower bound $\mathbf{r} + 1 = 3$ . We leave it as an exercise to show that this code is not equivalent to a cyclic code, showing that alternant codes are not always cyclic. $\square$
There are several important subclasses of alternant codes, besides the BCH codes. One of the most important subclasses consists of the Goppa codes, which we study next.
We will define Goppa codes without reference to alternant codes, and then show how these codes are, in fact, alternant codes.
Let $G(x)$ be a polynomial over $F_{q^m}$ . We will let $S_m$ denote the ring
of polynomials over $\mathbf{F}_{\mathbf{q}^{\mathbf{m}}}$ modulo $\mathbf{G}(\mathbf{x})$ . Note that $\mathbf{S}_{\mathbf{m}}$ is not a field unless $\mathbf{G}(\mathbf{x})$ is irreducible.
However, if $\mathbf{G}(\alpha) \neq 0$ , then the polynomial $x - \alpha$ is invertible in $S_{\mathbf{m}}$ . To see this, we divide $\mathbf{G}(x)$ by $x - \alpha$ , to get
With this in mind, we can define the expression $\frac{1}{x - \alpha}$ to be the polynomial in $S_{m}$ given by (8.3.1). Now we can define Goppa codes.

Theorem 8.3.2 The Goppa code $\Gamma(L, G)$ , where $\deg(G(x)) = r < n$ , has length $n = |\mathbf{L}|$ , dimension $k$ satisfying

In the case of binary Goppa codes, we can improve upon the lower bound on the minimum distance. Let $\mathbf{a} = \mathbf{a}_1 \cdots \mathbf{a}_n$ be a codeword in the binary Goppa code $\Gamma(L, G)$ . Assume that $w(\mathbf{a}) = w$ and that
Then if $\mathbf{f}_{\mathbf{a}}^{\prime}(\mathbf{x})$ denotes the derivative of $\mathbf{f}_{\mathbf{a}}(\mathbf{x})$ , we have
Moreover, since the polynomials $\mathbf{f}_{\mathbf{a}}(\mathbf{x})$ and $\mathbf{G}(\mathbf{x})$ have no common roots in any extension, they are relatively prime, and so

Now, since $\mathbf{q} = 2$ , the polynomial $\mathbf{f}_{\mathbf{a}}^{\prime}(\mathbf{x})$ has only even powers of $\mathbf{x}$ , and so $\mathbf{f}_{\mathbf{a}}^{\prime}(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{h}(\mathbf{x}^{2}) = [\mathbf{h}(\mathbf{x})]^{2}$ for some polynomial $\mathbf{h}(\mathbf{x})$ . Thus, if $\mathbf{G}^{*}(\mathbf{x})$ is the polynomial of smallest degree that is a perfect square and
is divisible by $G(x)$ , we see that $G(x) \mid f_{a}^{\prime}(x)$ if and only if $G^{*}(x) \mid f_{a}^{\prime}(x)$ . Hence,
Thus, we have the following result.

Theorem 8.3.3 Let $\Gamma(L, G)$ be a binary Goppa code. If $G^{*}(x)$ is the polynomial of smallest degree that is a perfect square and is divisible by $G(x)$ , then $\Gamma(L, G) = \Gamma(L, G^{*})$ . In particular, $\Gamma(L, G)$ has minimum distance at least $\deg(G^{*}(x)) + 1$ . $\square$
The following special case is of particular interest.

Corollary 8.3.4 Let $\Gamma(L, G)$ be a binary Goppa code, and suppose that the Goppa polynomial has no multiple roots in any extension field. Then $\Gamma(L, G) = \Gamma(L, G^2)$ . In particular, the minimum distance of $\Gamma(L, G)$ is at least $2\deg(G(x)) + 1$ . Hence, $\Gamma(L, G)$ can correct at least $\deg(G(x))$ errors. $\square$
A binary Goppa code whose Goppa polynomial has no multiple roots is called a separable Goppa code.

Example 8.3.2 Let $\mathbf{G}(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{x}^2 + \mathbf{x} + 1$ and $\mathbf{L} = \{0, 1, \omega, \ldots, \omega^6\} = \mathbf{F}_8$ . Thus, $\mathbf{q} = 2$ , $\mathbf{m} = 3$ , $\mathbf{r} = 2$ , $\mathbf{n} = 8$ , and $\omega$ is a primitive element of $\mathbf{F}_8$ . The zeros of $\mathbf{G}(\mathbf{x})$ do not lie in $\mathbf{F}_8$ . One way to see this (aside from direct substitution) is to observe that the polynomial $\mathbf{G}(\mathbf{x})$ is irreducible over $\mathbf{F}_2$ , and so according to Theorem 7.2.3, its splitting field is $\mathbf{F}_{2^2}$ . Hence, the roots of $\mathbf{G}(\mathbf{x})$ lie in $\mathbf{F}_{2^i}$ if and only if $i$ is a multiple of 2. (See Theorem 7.1.6.)
The parity check matrix of the Goppa code $\Gamma(L, G)$ is obtained from the matrix
Using the field table for $\mathbf{F_8}$ , we get

$$
\mathbf {H} = \left[ \begin{array}{c c c c c c c c} 1 & 1 & \omega^ {2} & \omega^ {4} & \omega^ {2} & \omega & \omega & \omega^ {4} \\ 0 & 1 & \omega^ {3} & \omega^ {6} & \omega^ {5} & \omega^ {5} & \omega^ {6} & \omega^ {3} \end{array} \right]
$$
Since this matrix has rank 6, it is a parity check matrix for $\Gamma(L, G)$ , which therefore has dimension $8 - 6 = 2$ . A little matrix manipulation gives

Example 8.3.3 Let $\mathbf{G}(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{x}^2 + 1$ , and $\mathbf{L} = \{0, \omega, \omega^2, \omega^3, \omega^5, \omega^6\} \subset \mathbf{F}_8$ where $\omega$ is a primitive element of $\mathbf{F}_8$ . Thus, $\mathbf{q} = 2$ , $\mathbf{m} = 3$ , $\mathbf{r} = 2$ and $\mathbf{n} = 6$ . Since $\mathbf{F}_8$ has characteristic 2, we have $\mathbf{G}(\mathbf{x}) = (\mathbf{x} + 1)^2$ , and according to Corollary 8.3.4, $\Gamma = \Gamma(\mathbf{L}, \mathbf{G}) = \Gamma(\mathbf{L}, \mathbf{x} + 1)$ . Hence, $\Gamma$ has length 6, dimension 3, and minimum distance 3. $\square$

We now wish to describe a decoding procedure, using the Euclidean algorithm, that applies to alternant codes and hence, in particular, to BCH codes and Goppa codes. Quoting from MacWilliams and Sloane (1977): "Nevertheless, decoding using the Euclidean algorithm is by far the simplest to understand, and is certainly at least comparable in speed with the other methods (for $n < 10^6$ ) and so it is the method we prefer."
Let us begin with a close look at the Euclidean algorithm.

The Euclidean algorithm is a procedure for computing the greatest common divisor of two integers or polynomials. We will confine our attention to polynomials, since that is our immediate concern. Let $f(x)$ and $g(x)$ be nonzero polynomials over the same field $F$ (finite or infinite), and assume that $\deg(g(x)) \leq \deg(f(x))$ . It will be convenient to let $f(x) = r_{-1}(x)$ and $g(x) = r_0(x)$ .

We have seen that a cyclic code can be defined by specifying the complete set of its zeros
where $Z$ is a union of cyclotomic cosets modulo $q$ . In the case of BCH codes, we specify a subset of the zeros, whose exponents are consecutive integers

and then obtain the set of all zeros by taking the union of all cyclotomic cosets containing any of the numbers $\mathbf{b}, \mathbf{b} + 1, \ldots, \mathbf{b} + \delta - 2$ . In the Reed-Solomon case, the cyclotomic cosets are singleton sets, and so the set $S$ is a complete set of zeros.

The point that we wish to make here is that the families of cyclic codes that we have studied so far are defined by choosing the zeros of their codes in a special way. As we shall see, this is also true of the quadratic residue codes.
Let us turn now to a brief discussion of quadratic residues. Proofs of the following results can be found in standard texts on number theory.
Definition Let $\mathbf{p}$ be an odd prime. If $(\mathbf{a}, \mathbf{p}) = 1$ , then $\mathbf{a}$ is a quadratic residue modulo $\mathbf{p}$ if there is a number $\mathbf{x}$ for which
If there is no number $x$ satisfying this congruence, then $a$ is a quadratic nonresidue modulo $p$ .
In other words, a is a quadratic residue modulo $p$ if $a$ has a square root modulo $p$ .

The set of quadratic residues modulo $\mathfrak{p}$ that lie in the set $\mathbf{Z}_{\mathbf{p}}^{*} = \{1,2,\ldots ,\mathbf{p - 1}\}$ will be denoted by QR. Similarly, the set of quadratic nonresidues that lie in $\mathbb{Z}_{\mathbf{p}}^{*} = \{1,2,\ldots ,\mathbf{p - 1}\}$ will be denoted by NQR. It is not hard to see that any quadratic residue modulo $\mathfrak{p}$ is congruent to a member of QR.

Now let us consider the minimum distance of a binary quadratic residue code. It is possible to show, using Corollary 4.3.16, that the minimum distance of a quadratic residue code is odd. We then have the following result, known as the square root bound. Unfortunately, this bound is often not very good.
Theorem 8.4.6 (The square root bound) The minimum distance $d$ of the binary codes $\mathbb{Q}(\mathfrak{p})$ and $\mathcal{N}(\mathfrak{p})$ satisfies
Proof. Let $\mathbf{c}(\mathbf{x})$ be a codeword in $\mathbb{Q}(\mathfrak{p})$ of minimum nonzero weight $\mathbf{d}$ . We leave it to the reader to show that if $\mathbf{u} \in \mathbf{NQR}$ , then
is a word of minimum weight $\mathbf{d}$ in $\mathcal{N}(\mathfrak{p})$ . Hence, the polynomial

The fact that $\mathbb{Q}(\mathfrak{p})$ has odd minimum weight implies that $\mathbf{p}(1) = \mathbf{c}(1)^2 \neq 0$ , and so $\mathbf{p}(\mathbf{x}) \neq 0$ . In summary, $\mathbf{p}(\mathbf{x})$ is nonzero, is divisible by $\mathbf{q}(\mathbf{x})\mathbf{n}(\mathbf{x})$ , and has degree at most $\mathbf{p} - 1$ , and so $\mathbf{p}(\mathbf{x})$ must be a nonzero scalar multiple of $\mathbf{q}(\mathbf{x})\mathbf{n}(\mathbf{x})$ . In particular, $\mathbf{p}(\mathbf{x})$ must have weight $\mathbf{p}$ .

It follows that the product $\mathbf{c}(\mathbf{x})\mathbf{c}(\mathbf{x}^{\mathbf{u}})$ has weight at least $\mathbf{p}$ . But $\mathbf{c}(\mathbf{x})$ and $\mathbf{c}(\mathbf{x}^{\mathbf{u}})$ have $\mathbf{d}$ nonzero terms, and so this product can have no more than $\mathbf{d}^2$ nonzero terms. Therefore, $\mathbf{d}^2 \geq \mathbf{p}$ .

If $p = 8m - 1$ , then according to part 5) of Theorem 8.4.2, we can take $u = -1$ . But $c(x)c(x^{-1})$ has, among its at most $d^2$ nonzero terms, $d$ terms of the form $a_i x^i x^{-i}$ , which are constants, and which therefore collect into a single term. Hence, the product $c(x)c(x^{-1})$ has weight at most $d^2 - d + 1$ , and so $d^2 - d + 1 \geq p$ .

Example 8.4.6 The binary quadratic residue codes of length $\mathbf{p} = 7$ have minimum distance $\mathbf{d}$ satisfying $\mathbf{d}^2 - \mathbf{d} + 1 \geq 7$ . Hence, $\mathbf{d} \geq 3$ . As we have seen, the binary [7,4] quadratic residue code has minimum distance $\mathbf{d} = 3$ . $\square$

Definition A group is a nonempty set $G$ , together with a binary operation $*$ that satisfies the following properties.

Definition A group $G$ is abelian, or commutative, if $a * b = b * a$ , for all $a, b \in G$ .

We will often denote the action of $*$ by juxtaposition, thus writing $a * b$ simply as $ab$ . When a group is abelian, it is customary to denote the operation by $+$ , thus writing $a * b$ as $a + b$ .
Definition A subgroup S of a group G is a subset of G that is a group in its own right, using the same operation as defined on G. $\square$
Example A.1.1 The set $\mathbb{Z}_{\mathbf{n}} = \{0,1,\ldots ,\mathbf{n} - 1\}$ is an abelian group under addition modulo $\mathbf{n}$
A group $G$ is finite if it contains only a finite number of elements. The cardinality of a finite group $G$ is called its order and is denoted by $o(G)$ .

If $\mathbf{a} \in \mathbf{G}$ , and if $\mathbf{a}^{\mathbf{k}} = \mathbf{e}$ for some integer $\mathbf{k}$ , then we say that $\mathbf{k}$ is an exponent of $\mathbf{a}$ . The smallest positive exponent for $\mathbf{a} \in \mathbf{G}$ is called the order of $\mathbf{a}$ and is denoted by $o(\mathbf{a})$ .
Theorem A.1.1 Let $G$ be a group and let $a \in G$ . Then $k$ is an exponent of $a$ if and only if $k$ is a multiple of the order $o(a)$ . $\square$
Theorem A.1.2 Let $G$ be a finite group.

list_items: 1) The order of any subgroup of $\mathbf{G}$ divides the order of $\mathbf{G}$ .
2) The order of any element of $G$ divides the order of $G$ .

text: If $a$ and $b$ are integers, we denote their greatest common divisor by $(a, b)$ . If $(a, b) = 1$ , we say that $a$ and $b$ are relatively prime. The Euler phi function $\phi$ is defined by letting $\phi(n)$ be the number of positive integers less than or equal to $n$ that are relatively prime to $n$ .
Two integers a and b are congruent modulo n, written
if $\mathbf{a} - \mathbf{b}$ is divisible by $\mathbf{n}$ . Theorem A.1.2 can be used to prove the following important theorem.

Proof. The set $\mathbf{G} = \{\mathbf{b} \in \mathbb{Z}_{\mathbf{n}} \mid (\mathbf{b}, \mathbf{n}) = 1\}$ is a group of order $\phi(\mathbf{n})$ under multiplication modulo $\mathbf{n}$ . For if $\mathbf{b}, \mathbf{c} \in \mathbf{G}$ , then so is $\mathbf{bc}$ . Also, if $\mathbf{b} \in \mathbf{G}$ , then there exists $\mathbf{p}, \mathbf{q}$ such that $\mathbf{pb} + \mathbf{qn} = 1$ , and so

$\mathbf{pb} \equiv 1 \bmod \mathfrak{n}$ . That is, $\mathbf{b}^{-1} = \mathbf{p} \bmod \mathfrak{n}$ . Thus, Theorem A.1.2 implies that $\mathbf{a}^{\phi(\mathfrak{n})} \equiv 1 \bmod \mathfrak{n}$ , for all $\mathbf{a} \in \mathbf{G}$ .

If $\mathbf{a} \notin G$ , then there exists an $\mathbf{a}' \in G$ for which $\mathbf{a}' \equiv \mathbf{a} \mod n$ . In other words, $\mathbf{a}' = \mathbf{a} + k\mathbf{n}$ for some integer $k$ , and so $(\mathbf{a}, \mathbf{n}) = 1$ implies $(\mathbf{a}', \mathbf{n}) = 1$ . Thus, we have $\mathbf{a}^{\phi(\mathbf{n})} \equiv (\mathbf{a}')^{\phi(\mathbf{n})} \equiv 1 \mod n$ .

Theorem A.1.5 Every subgroup of a cyclic group is cyclic. $\square$
The following theorem summarizes some key results about finite cyclic groups.

In particular, $\mathbf{a}^{\mathbf{k}}$ generates $G = \langle \mathbf{a} \rangle$ if and only if $(n, k) = 1$ .

Definition A ring is a nonempty set $\mathbf{R}$ , together with two binary operations, called addition (denoted by $+$ ), and multiplication (denoted by juxtaposition), satisfying the following properties.

list_items: 1) $\mathbf{R}$ is an abelian group under the operation $+$ .
2) (associativity) For all $\mathbf{a}, \mathbf{b}, \mathbf{c} \in \mathbb{R}$ ,

text:
$$
(a + b) c = a c + a b \quad a n d \quad c (a + b) = c a + c b
$$

list_items: 1) A ring $\mathbf{R}$ is called a ring with identity if there exists an element $1 \in \mathbf{R}$ for which $\mathbf{a}1 = 1\mathbf{a} = \mathbf{a}$ , for all $\mathbf{a} \in \mathbf{R}$ .
2) A ring $\mathbf{R}$ is called a commutative ring if multiplication is commutative, that is, if $\mathbf{ab} = \mathbf{ba}$ for all $\mathbf{a}, \mathbf{b} \in \mathbf{R}$ .

3) A ring $\mathbf{R}$ with identity is called an integral domain if it is commutative, and if there are no zero divisors in $\mathbf{R}$ , where a nonzero element $a \in \mathbf{R}$ is a zero divisor if there exists a nonzero element $b \in \mathbf{R}$ for which $ab = 0$ .
4) A ring $\mathbf{R}$ with identity $1 \neq 0$ is called a field if the nonzero elements of $\mathbf{R}$ form an abelian group under multiplication. $\square$

text: Theorem A.1.7 Every finite integral domain is a field. $\square$
Definition A subring of a ring $\mathbf{R}$ is a subset $S$ of $\mathbf{R}$ that is a ring in its own right, using the same operations as defined on $\mathbf{R}$ . $\square$

Intuitively speaking, a homomorphism between two rings $\mathbf{R}$ and $\mathbf{S}$ is a function $\psi: \mathbf{R} \to \mathbf{S}$ that preserves the operations of addition and multiplication. More specifically, we have the following definitions.
Definition Let $\mathbf{R}$ and $\mathbf{S}$ be rings (or fields). A function $\psi: \mathbf{R} \to \mathbf{S}$ is a homomorphism if, for all $\mathbf{a}, \mathbf{b} \in \mathbf{R}$ ,

A homomorphism that is also a bijection (one-to-one and onto) is called an isomorphism. If $\psi: \mathbf{R} \to \mathbf{S}$ is an isomorphism, we say that $\mathbf{R}$ and $\mathbf{S}$ are isomorphic. $\square$

When two rings (or fields) are isomorphic, then from the point of view of their algebraic structure, they are essentially the same. Put another way, isomorphic rings (or fields) can be thought of merely as different concrete representations of the same abstract ring (or field).
Definition A subset I of a ring $\mathbf{R}$ is called an ideal if it satisfies

list_items: 1) $\mathbf{a}, \mathbf{b} \in \mathbf{I}$ implies $\mathbf{a} - \mathbf{b} \in \mathbf{I}$ .
2) $\mathbf{a} \in \mathbb{R}, \mathbf{i} \in \mathbb{I} \Rightarrow \mathbf{ai} \in \mathbb{I}$ and $\mathbf{ia} \in \mathbb{I}$ .

text: If S is a nonempty subset of a ring R, then the ideal generated by S is defined to be the smallest ideal I of R containing S. If R is a commutative ring with identity, and if $\mathbf{a}\in \mathbb{R}$ , then the ideal generated by a is the set
Any ideal of the form $\langle \mathbf{a} \rangle$ is called a principal ideal.
Definition A ring $\mathbf{R}$ is called a principal ideal domain if every ideal of $\mathbf{R}$ is principal. $\square$
Suppose that $\mathbf{R}$ is a ring and $\mathbf{I}$ is an ideal in $\mathbf{R}$ . Then for each $\mathbf{a} \in \mathbf{R}$ , we can form the set

which is called a coset of I. It can be shown that $\mathbf{a} + \mathbf{I} = \mathbf{b} + \mathbf{I}$ if and only if $\mathbf{a} - \mathbf{b} \in \mathbf{I}$ , and that any two cosets $\mathbf{a} + \mathbf{I}$ and $\mathbf{b} + \mathbf{I}$ are either disjoint or identical. Furthermore, the collection of all (distinct) cosets can be made into a ring itself with addition and multiplication defined by
The ring of all cosets of $\mathbf{I}$ is called a factor ring and is denoted by $\mathbf{R} / \mathbf{I}$ , read “ $\mathbf{R}$ mod $\mathbf{I}$ .”
Example A.1.2 The factor ring $\mathbb{Z} / \langle n \rangle$ is isomorphic to the ring $\mathbb{Z}_n$ , under addition and multiplication modulo $n$ . The ring $\mathbb{Z}_n$ is a field if
and only if n is a prime. $\square$

Definition An ideal $\mathbf{I}$ is maximal if $\mathbf{I} \neq \mathbf{R}$ , and if whenever $\mathbf{J}$ is an ideal for which $\mathbf{I} \subseteq \mathbf{J} \subseteq \mathbf{R}$ , then $\mathbf{J} = \mathbf{I}$ or $\mathbf{J} = \mathbf{R}$ . $\square$
Theorem A.1.8 Let $\mathbf{R}$ be a commutative ring with identity. Then the factor ring $\mathbf{R} / \mathbf{I}$ is a field if and only if $\mathbf{I}$ is a maximal ideal. $\square$

is the set of all cosets of $\mathbf{I} = \langle \mathbf{p}(\mathbf{x})\rangle$

Thus, we may identify the factor ring $\mathbf{K}$ with the set of all polynomials of degree less than $d$ , with addition and multiplication performed modulo $\mathfrak{p}(\mathbf{x})$ .
If $\mathbf{R}$ is a ring, and if there exists a positive integer $n$ for which

$$
\mathrm {n a} = \underbrace {\mathrm {a} + \mathrm {a} + \cdots + \mathrm {a}} _ {\textbf {n t i m e s}} = 0
$$

for all $a \in \mathbb{R}$ , then the smallest such positive integer is called the characteristic of the ring and is denoted by $\text{char}(\mathbb{R})$ . If no such integer exists, we say that $\mathbb{R}$ has characteristic 0. For instance, the ring $\mathbb{Z}_n$ has characteristic 0. On the other hand, the ring of integers $\mathbb{Z}$ has characteristic 0.
Theorem A.1.9 Any integral domain has either characteristic 0 or a prime characteristic. In particular, a finite field has prime characteristic.

Proof. First we observe that the characteristic of a ring with identity is the smallest positive integer $n$ for which $n1 = 0$ . Now suppose that $R$ is an integral domain of finite characteristic $n$ . If $n = pq$ , where $p, q < n$ , then $pq1 = 0$ . Hence, $(p1)(q1) = 0$ , implying that $p1 = 0$ or $q1 = 0$ . In either case, we have a contradiction to the fact that $n$ is the smallest positive integer such that $n1 = 0$ . Hence, $n$ must be prime. Since a finite field cannot have characteristic 0 (why not?), the second part follows from the first.
The following result will be of considerable importance to us.
